title: Tekton Buildah on BuildPlatform linux/amd64 building with --arch=ppc64le exits with process exited with error: fork/exec /bin/sh: exec format errorsubprocess exited with status 1
body:
**Description**

I have a Tekton Pipeline on an openshift cluster based on amd64 hardware. It builds a Dockerfile for that architecture very well. Now then, I have made a mod with the purpose of making this pipeline build the same Dockerfile into an image for the ppc64le architecture using the --arch option. The dockerfile builds from jboss-eap-7/eap73-openj9-11-runtime-openshift-rhel8 (using --from on this image that I have imported, so I can play with it). This base image has been build for ppc64le and s390x, and not for amd. The build exits with fork/exec /bin/sh: exec format errorsubprocess exited with status 1

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**
1. modify the tekton buildah task to add the --arch=ppc64le option, (sample attached);
2. build FROM jboss-eap-7/eap73-openj9-11-runtime-openshift-rhel8
3. let the dockerfile make a directory


**Describe the results you received:**

The build exits as follows:

[ppc-build : build] STEP 5/21: RUN mkdir -pv /opt/eap/standalone/deployments &&     mkdir -pv /opt/Tivoli/monitoring/logs &&     mkdir -pv /data/weblogic/pnst/logs &&    mkdir -pv /weblogic 
[ppc-build : build] process exited with error: fork/exec /bin/sh: exec format errorsubprocess exited with status 1
[ppc-build : build] error building at STEP "RUN mkdir -pv /opt/eap/standalone/deployments &&     mkdir -pv /opt/Tivoli/monitoring/logs &&     mkdir -pv /data/weblogic/pnst/logs &&       mkdir -pv /weblogic": exit status 1

The logs I have attached

**Describe the results you expected:**

I interpret the manual text for this option:
"Set the architecture of the image to be built, and that of the base image to be pulled, if the build uses one, to the provided value instead of using the architecture of the build host. (Examples: arm, arm64, 386, amd64, ppc64le, s390x)"

So, I interpret it that I specified the image to be build via the --arch option, and the image that is pulled is available for ppc64le, so it will pull it. So, it should then build the Dockerfile to ppc64le instead of the amd64 architecture of the host.

Is that a correct interpretation?

**Output of `rpm -q buildah` or `apt list buildah`:**

```
[ppc-build : rpm] buildah-1.23.1-1.fc35.x86_64
```

**Output of `buildah version`:**

```
[ppc-build : version] Version:         1.23.1
[ppc-build : version] Go Version:      go1.16.8
[ppc-build : version] Image Spec:      1.0.1-dev
[ppc-build : version] Runtime Spec:    1.0.2-dev
[ppc-build : version] CNI Spec:        0.4.0
[ppc-build : version] libcni Version:  v0.8.1
[ppc-build : version] image Version:   5.16.0
[ppc-build : version] Git Commit:      
[ppc-build : version] Built:           Tue Sep 28 18:26:37 2021
[ppc-build : version] OS/Arch:         linux/amd64
[ppc-build : version] BuildPlatform:   linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman version` if reporting a `podman build` issue:**

```
Not Applicable
```

**Output of `cat /etc/*release`:**

```
[ppc-build : release] + cat /etc/fedora-release /etc/os-release /etc/redhat-release /etc/system-release
[ppc-build : release] Fedora release 35 (Thirty Five)
[ppc-build : release] NAME="Fedora Linux"
[ppc-build : release] VERSION="35 (Container Image)"
[ppc-build : release] ID=fedora
[ppc-build : release] VERSION_ID=35
[ppc-build : release] VERSION_CODENAME=""
[ppc-build : release] PLATFORM_ID="platform:f35"
[ppc-build : release] PRETTY_NAME="Fedora Linux 35 (Container Image)"
[ppc-build : release] ANSI_COLOR="0;38;2;60;110;180"
[ppc-build : release] LOGO=fedora-logo-icon
[ppc-build : release] CPE_NAME="cpe:/o:fedoraproject:fedora:35"
[ppc-build : release] HOME_URL="https://fedoraproject.org/"
[ppc-build : release] DOCUMENTATION_URL="https://docs.fedoraproject.org/en-US/fedora/f35/system-administrators-guide/"
[ppc-build : release] SUPPORT_URL="https://ask.fedoraproject.org/"
[ppc-build : release] BUG_REPORT_URL="https://bugzilla.redhat.com/"
[ppc-build : release] REDHAT_BUGZILLA_PRODUCT="Fedora"
[ppc-build : release] REDHAT_BUGZILLA_PRODUCT_VERSION=35
[ppc-build : release] REDHAT_SUPPORT_PRODUCT="Fedora"
[ppc-build : release] REDHAT_SUPPORT_PRODUCT_VERSION=35
[ppc-build : release] PRIVACY_POLICY_URL="https://fedoraproject.org/wiki/Legal:PrivacyPolicy"
[ppc-build : release] VARIANT="Container Image"
[ppc-build : release] VARIANT_ID=container
[ppc-build : release] Fedora release 35 (Thirty Five)
[ppc-build : release] Fedora release 35 (Thirty Five)
[ppc-build : release] + uname -a
[ppc-build : release] Linux pnst-ppc-xmqml-ppc-build-96mgz-pod-cllgv 3.10.0-1160.45.1.el7.x86_64 #1 SMP Fri Sep 24 10:17:16 UTC 2021 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux
```

**Output of `uname -a`:**

```
(paste your output here)
```

**Output of `cat /etc/containers/storage.conf`:**

```
[ppc-build : release] + cat /etc/containers/storage.conf
[ppc-build : release] # This file is is the configuration file for all tools
[ppc-build : release] # that use the containers/storage library.
[ppc-build : release] # See man 5 containers-storage.conf for more information
[ppc-build : release] # The "container storage" table contains all of the server options.
[ppc-build : release] [storage]
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # Default Storage Driver, Must be set for proper operation.
[ppc-build : release] driver = "overlay"
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # Temporary storage location
[ppc-build : release] runroot = "/run/containers/storage"
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # Primary Read/Write location of container storage
[ppc-build : release] graphroot = "/var/lib/containers/storage"
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # Storage path for rootless users
[ppc-build : release] #
[ppc-build : release] # rootless_storage_path = "$HOME/.local/share/containers/storage"
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] [storage.options]
[ppc-build : release] # Storage options to be passed to underlying storage drivers
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # AdditionalImageStores is used to pass paths to additional Read/Only image stores
[ppc-build : release] # Must be comma separated list.
[ppc-build : release] additionalimagestores = [
[ppc-build : release] "/var/lib/shared",
[ppc-build : release] ]
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # Remap-UIDs/GIDs is the mapping from UIDs/GIDs as they should appear inside of
[ppc-build : release] # a container, to the UIDs/GIDs as they should appear outside of the container,
[ppc-build : release] # and the length of the range of UIDs/GIDs.  Additional mapped sets can be
[ppc-build : release] # listed and will be heeded by libraries, but there are limits to the number of
[ppc-build : release] # mappings which the kernel will allow when you later attempt to run a
[ppc-build : release] # container.
[ppc-build : release] #
[ppc-build : release] # remap-uids = 0:1668442479:65536
[ppc-build : release] # remap-gids = 0:1668442479:65536
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # Remap-User/Group is a user name which can be used to look up one or more UID/GID
[ppc-build : release] # ranges in the /etc/subuid or /etc/subgid file.  Mappings are set up starting
[ppc-build : release] # with an in-container ID of 0 and then a host-level ID taken from the lowest
[ppc-build : release] # range that matches the specified name, and using the length of that range.
[ppc-build : release] # Additional ranges are then assigned, using the ranges which specify the
[ppc-build : release] # lowest host-level IDs first, to the lowest not-yet-mapped in-container ID,
[ppc-build : release] # until all of the entries have been used for maps.
[ppc-build : release] #
[ppc-build : release] # remap-user = "containers"
[ppc-build : release] # remap-group = "containers"
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # Root-auto-userns-user is a user name which can be used to look up one or more UID/GID
[ppc-build : release] # ranges in the /etc/subuid and /etc/subgid file.  These ranges will be partitioned
[ppc-build : release] # to containers configured to create automatically a user namespace.  Containers
[ppc-build : release] # configured to automatically create a user namespace can still overlap with containers
[ppc-build : release] # having an explicit mapping set.
[ppc-build : release] # This setting is ignored when running as rootless.
[ppc-build : release] # root-auto-userns-user = "storage"
[ppc-build : release] #
[ppc-build : release] # Auto-userns-min-size is the minimum size for a user namespace created automatically.
[ppc-build : release] # auto-userns-min-size=1024
[ppc-build : release] #
[ppc-build : release] # Auto-userns-max-size is the minimum size for a user namespace created automatically.
[ppc-build : release] # auto-userns-max-size=65536
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] [storage.options.overlay]
[ppc-build : release] # ignore_chown_errors can be set to allow a non privileged user running with
[ppc-build : release] # a single UID within a user namespace to run containers. The user can pull
[ppc-build : release] # and use any image even those with multiple uids.  Note multiple UIDs will be
[ppc-build : release] # squashed down to the default uid in the container.  These images will have no
[ppc-build : release] # separation between the users in the container. Only supported for the overlay
[ppc-build : release] # and vfs drivers.
[ppc-build : release] #ignore_chown_errors = "false"
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # Inodes is used to set a maximum inodes of the container image.
[ppc-build : release] # inodes = ""
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # Path to an helper program to use for mounting the file system instead of mounting it
[ppc-build : release] # directly.
[ppc-build : release] mount_program = "/usr/bin/fuse-overlayfs"
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # mountopt specifies comma separated list of extra mount options
[ppc-build : release] mountopt = "nodev,fsync=0"
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # Set to skip a PRIVATE bind mount on the storage home directory.
[ppc-build : release] # skip_mount_home = "false"
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # Size is used to set a maximum size of the container image.
[ppc-build : release] # size = ""
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # ForceMask specifies the permissions mask that is used for new files and
[ppc-build : release] # directories.
[ppc-build : release] #
[ppc-build : release] # The values "shared" and "private" are accepted.
[ppc-build : release] # Octal permission masks are also accepted.
[ppc-build : release] #
[ppc-build : release] #  "": No value specified.
[ppc-build : release] #     All files/directories, get set with the permissions identified within the
[ppc-build : release] #     image.
[ppc-build : release] #  "private": it is equivalent to 0700.
[ppc-build : release] #     All files/directories get set with 0700 permissions.  The owner has rwx
[ppc-build : release] #     access to the files. No other users on the system can access the files.
[ppc-build : release] #     This setting could be used with networked based homedirs.
[ppc-build : release] #  "shared": it is equivalent to 0755.
[ppc-build : release] #     The owner has rwx access to the files and everyone else can read, access
[ppc-build : release] #     and execute them. This setting is useful for sharing containers storage
[ppc-build : release] #     with other users.  For instance have a storage owned by root but shared
[ppc-build : release] #     to rootless users as an additional store.
[ppc-build : release] #     NOTE:  All files within the image are made readable and executable by any
[ppc-build : release] #     user on the system. Even /etc/shadow within your image is now readable by
[ppc-build : release] #     any user.
[ppc-build : release] #
[ppc-build : release] #   OCTAL: Users can experiment with other OCTAL Permissions.
[ppc-build : release] #
[ppc-build : release] #  Note: The force_mask Flag is an experimental feature, it could change in the
[ppc-build : release] #  future.  When "force_mask" is set the original permission mask is stored in
[ppc-build : release] #  the "user.containers.override_stat" xattr and the "mount_program" option must
[ppc-build : release] #  be specified. Mount programs like "/usr/bin/fuse-overlayfs" present the
[ppc-build : release] #  extended attribute permissions to processes within containers rather then the
[ppc-build : release] #  "force_mask"  permissions.
[ppc-build : release] #
[ppc-build : release] # force_mask = ""
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] [storage.options.thinpool]
[ppc-build : release] # Storage Options for thinpool
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # autoextend_percent determines the amount by which pool needs to be
[ppc-build : release] # grown. This is specified in terms of % of pool size. So a value of 20 means
[ppc-build : release] # that when threshold is hit, pool will be grown by 20% of existing
[ppc-build : release] # pool size.
[ppc-build : release] # autoextend_percent = "20"
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # autoextend_threshold determines the pool extension threshold in terms
[ppc-build : release] # of percentage of pool size. For example, if threshold is 60, that means when
[ppc-build : release] # pool is 60% full, threshold has been hit.
[ppc-build : release] # autoextend_threshold = "80"
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # basesize specifies the size to use when creating the base device, which
[ppc-build : release] # limits the size of images and containers.
[ppc-build : release] # basesize = "10G"
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # blocksize specifies a custom blocksize to use for the thin pool.
[ppc-build : release] # blocksize="64k"
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # directlvm_device specifies a custom block storage device to use for the
[ppc-build : release] # thin pool. Required if you setup devicemapper.
[ppc-build : release] # directlvm_device = ""
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # directlvm_device_force wipes device even if device already has a filesystem.
[ppc-build : release] # directlvm_device_force = "True"
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # fs specifies the filesystem type to use for the base device.
[ppc-build : release] # fs="xfs"
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # log_level sets the log level of devicemapper.
[ppc-build : release] # 0: LogLevelSuppress 0 (Default)
[ppc-build : release] # 2: LogLevelFatal
[ppc-build : release] # 3: LogLevelErr
[ppc-build : release] # 4: LogLevelWarn
[ppc-build : release] # 5: LogLevelNotice
[ppc-build : release] # 6: LogLevelInfo
[ppc-build : release] # 7: LogLevelDebug
[ppc-build : release] # log_level = "7"
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # min_free_space specifies the min free space percent in a thin pool require for
[ppc-build : release] # new device creation to succeed. Valid values are from 0% - 99%.
[ppc-build : release] # Value 0% disables
[ppc-build : release] # min_free_space = "10%"
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # mkfsarg specifies extra mkfs arguments to be used when creating the base
[ppc-build : release] # device.
[ppc-build : release] # mkfsarg = ""
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # metadata_size is used to set the `pvcreate --metadatasize` options when
[ppc-build : release] # creating thin devices. Default is 128k
[ppc-build : release] # metadata_size = ""
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # Size is used to set a maximum size of the container image.
[ppc-build : release] # size = ""
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # use_deferred_removal marks devicemapper block device for deferred removal.
[ppc-build : release] # If the thinpool is in use when the driver attempts to remove it, the driver
[ppc-build : release] # tells the kernel to remove it as soon as possible. Note this does not free
[ppc-build : release] # up the disk space, use deferred deletion to fully remove the thinpool.
[ppc-build : release] # use_deferred_removal = "True"
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # use_deferred_deletion marks thinpool device for deferred deletion.
[ppc-build : release] # If the device is busy when the driver attempts to delete it, the driver
[ppc-build : release] # will attempt to delete device every 30 seconds until successful.
[ppc-build : release] # If the program using the driver exits, the driver will continue attempting
[ppc-build : release] # to cleanup the next time the driver is used. Deferred deletion permanently
[ppc-build : release] # deletes the device and all data stored in device will be lost.
[ppc-build : release] # use_deferred_deletion = "True"
[ppc-build : release] 
[ppc-build : release] # xfs_nospace_max_retries specifies the maximum number of retries XFS should
[ppc-build : release] # attempt to complete IO when ENOSPC (no space) error is returned by
[ppc-build : release] # underlying storage device.
[ppc-build : release] # xfs_nospace_max_retries = "0"
```

comments.body:
As extra info, I was not able to build the image on my x86 based laptop, straight via podman or buildah:

podman build --arch=ppc64le -t pnst-on-ppc .
buildah bud --arch=ppc64le -t ppc-pnst .

Is my understanding of the use case correct?

@nalind PTAL
In order to build on a different arch then native, you need to have the qemu-user-static package installed.  OpenShift might have some more advanced features for this.

@kitty-catt Your interpretation of what buildah does when you use the `--arch` flag is quite correct.  There's a limitation, though:  while buildah's happy to build images for non-default architectures, it doesn't have the ability to run binaries that were compiled for foreign architectures on its own.  This means that, unless emulation is configured so that the kernel will use an interpreter to run binaries whose headers identify them as being built for foreign architectures (this is most easily done by installing a `qemu-user-static` package, as @rhatdan mentioned), RUN instructions will fail with the "exec format error" message you're seeing.
I'm not aware of any plans to have OpenShift set this up for worker nodes, and I'm afraid I don't have any ready solutions to offer, either.
I know that Red Hat has rejected support for qemu-user-static.  So the question would be if this could be provided from EPEL. (If that would even be possible.)

Well, I've been poking at it off and on for a while, and https://github.com/nalind/fedora-qemu-user-static/blob/main/daemonset.yml seems to more or less work on my CodeReady Containers setup.  I would hesitate to deploy it in a cluster that couldn't be completely destroyed and recreated as easily, though.

This morning I build my first ppc image after installing qemu-user-static. Thanks a lot, Nalin. 
My Tekton pipeline runs the quay.io/buildah image. Would it suffice to install the qemu-user-static package there? In that case I can make a request for it and see what happens. Or should it be installed at the level of the OS of the worker node? For openshift that would be coreos.

It turns out that I could add the package to this image via the following Dockerfile. Let me test it in the pipeline and see what happens:

    FROM quay.io/buildah/stable:v1.23.1
    USER root
    RUN yum install -y qemu-user-static

I have added the package to the quay.io/buildah image and added some diagnostics:

    [ppc-build : diagnostics] + echo ''
    [ppc-build : diagnostics] + echo 'qemu-user-static rpm'
    [ppc-build : diagnostics] + /usr/bin/rpm -q qemu-user-static
    [ppc-build : diagnostics] qemu-user-static rpm
    [ppc-build : diagnostics] qemu-user-static-6.1.0-10.fc35.x86_64
    [ppc-build : diagnostics] + echo ''

The pod that executes the Tekton Task with the qemu-user-static enhanced buildah container image still exits:

    [ppc-build : build] STEP 5/21: RUN mkdir -pv /opt/eap/standalone/deployments &&     mkdir -pv /opt/Tivoli/monitoring/logs &&     mkdir -pv /data/weblogic/pnst/logs &&        mkdir -pv /weblogic 
    [ppc-build : build] process exited with error: fork/exec /bin/sh: exec format errorsubprocess exited with status 1
    [ppc-build : build] error building at STEP "RUN mkdir -pv /opt/eap/standalone/deployments &&     mkdir -pv /opt/Tivoli/monitoring/logs &&     mkdir -pv /data/weblogic/pnst/logs &&   mkdir -pv /weblogic": exit status 1

I am very gratefull that I can build the application image for ppc on my intel laptop with RHEL 8.4.

Does anyone have suggestions on what I could try?

> This morning I build my first ppc image after installing qemu-user-static. Thanks a lot, Nalin. My Tekton pipeline runs the quay.io/buildah image. Would it suffice to install the qemu-user-static package there? In that case I can make a request for it and see what happens. Or should it be installed at the level of the OS of the worker node? For openshift that would be coreos.

The interpreters in the package have to be registered with the `binfmt_misc` kernel module so that the kernel will know to use them to run binaries whose headers indicate that they were built for architectures other than the default one.  That registration has to be done at the node level, which is a privileged operation, so having the package in the container image isn't sufficient by itself.

The Fedora package, for example, drops configuration files into `/usr/lib/binfmt.d` that the `systemd-binfmt` service consults at startup to perform that registration step.

OpenShift uses RHEL CoreOS on nodes that it provisions, and the qemu-user-static package isn't available in RHEL, so it's probably not going to become an option there.  Using emulation is costly enough that I expect most installations of any size will still prefer to use native hardware.

Using oc debug node I verified "yum provides qemu-user-static" and you are 200% right, the package is not available in the coreos repos / subscription-manager. 

In my mind I allready had a plan to install the package on one of the nodes of the cluster, and schedule the build there via a nodeSelector. 

Such an approach would not be the Openshift way where the whole of CoreOS is configured at installation time, and is upgraded as a whole when a cluster gets updated. 

I could image that RH would support an "upgrade as a whole" configuration for the package (there is probably a lot more to it). In order for that to happen qemu-user-static would have to get into the packages for rhcos. 

The use case would be to allow development on low cost clusters in the cloud, and next have the build move to more expensive environments based on ppc or s390. It could make ppc/s390 contracts more attrative.

The package itself is present on Fedora 35 (not RHEL 8.4) where I build the image for ppc. So, it should not be too hard to add it rhcos I believe.

I can imagine the Red Hat thinks "the package will make the coreos heavy and less secure", but would not know.

I could raise a support case for it with Red Hat and see what they think.

Do you have considerations that I could add to the support case?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
